articles.summary:
The Mophie Roam 3-in-1 Travel Charger offers a premium, portable solution for Apple users, featuring dedicated wireless pads for iPhones, Apple Watches, and AirPods in a compact design.

### Key Points
- The charger includes a 25W pad for iPhones, a 7.5W pad for Apple Watches, and a 5W pad for AirPods.
- It utilizes proprietary StealthCharge Technology to dissipate heat quietly without the need for internal fans.
- The kit is MagSafe and Qi 2.2-certified and includes a 40W USB-C wall adapter with international plug prongs.
- It features a foldable, travel-friendly design that fits into a minimalist leather pouch for portability.
- The 2026 fourth-generation model is currently available in Black or Cream for $149.95.

### Why it Matters
This product highlights the disparity in high-quality, all-in-one charging accessories between the Apple and Google Pixel ecosystems. While Apple users benefit from comprehensive, officially certified travel kits, Pixel users currently lack similar integrated solutions due to hardware fragmentation and a lack of standardized watch charging support.

articles.title: As Google sits back and watches, terrible ads are ruining Android’s free app ecosystem
articles.author: Jerry Hildenbrand
articles.content:
As Google sits back and watches, terrible ads are ruining Android's free app ecosystem
Jul 18, 2026 — 7:00 AM ET
Remember the early days of Android? It felt like the Wild West in the best way possible. You could hop onto the Play Store, type in a random app or game you wanted — like a flashlight, a simple unit converter, or a quirky indie puzzle game — and download a completely free app that did exactly what it promised.
Sure, there might have been a tiny, unobtrusive banner ad at the bottom of the screen, but it was a fair trade. You got a great tool, the developer made a few pennies, and everyone went home happy. Fast forward to today, and that beautiful, open ecosystem is actively suffocating.
If you download a free app on Android right now, you probably aren’t getting something free and useful (or fun); you’re getting an obstacle course. The free app ecosystem has degenerated into an absolute minefield of user-hostile advertising. And while developers and the ad networks themselves implement these nightmare tactics, the ultimate blame lies squarely at Google’s feet, which seems perfectly content to sit back, count its billions, and watch the platform burn.
Do you think ads are ruining free Android apps?
The anatomy of a modern mobile ad night-terror
We aren’t talking about simple banner ads anymore. The monetization strategies allowed on Android today feel less like business and more like psychological warfare. Some developers and advertisers even try to skirt the rules and go a step further.
If you’ve used a free app recently, you’ve definitely run into these “greatest hits” of spammy ads:
- The un-X-able interstitial: You open something like a basic calculator app, and boom — a full-screen video ad for a mobile strategy game slaps you in the face. You look for the “X” to close it, but it’s deliberately hidden, microscopic, or delayed by a fake countdown timer. Accidentally tap anywhere else? Congratulations, you’ve just been redirected to a sketchy landing page.
- The psychological trap (playable ads): These are interactive mini-games that present a completely fake gameplay scenario (usually a puzzle that involves pulling pins to save someone from lava). They are intentionally designed to look easy, so you tap the screen, only for the ad to register that tap as a click-through to the Play Store.
- The notification hijack: Some apps have the absolute audacity to push spam straight to your Android notification shade when the app isn’t even open, buzzing your pocket just to tell you there’s a “special bonus” waiting for you in a game you haven’t played in three weeks.
It’s exhausting. It turns a quick, two-second digital task into a multi-step battle against dark UI patterns designed to trick you into doing things you didn’t intend.
The death of the casual indie dev
The tragic irony here is that these toxic ads aren’t even saving the independent developers they were supposed to support.
Because the mobile ad market is dominated by massive, predatory ad networks, the payout per impression for normal, non-intrusive banner ads has tanked. To make any real money, small-time developers are practically forced to use aggressive ad SDKs (Software Development Kits) from major ad brokers. These SDKs are essentially black boxes that inject these horrible, flashing, high-volume video ads into the software.
If a developer refuses to ruin their app with these practices, they can’t compete. They get buried by the algorithm. The result? Talented indie creators are leaving the ecosystem entirely, leaving behind a vacuum filled by low-effort, template-based “copycat” apps designed solely to harvest user data and force-feed ads.
Where is Google? (Spoiler — it’s counting the money)
This brings us to the core of the problem: Google is the landlord of this digital slum.
Google owns Android. Google dictates the Play Store policies. More importantly, Google owns Google Ads and AdMob, the massive infrastructure powering a huge chunk of this very inventory. It possesses all the data, all the engineering power, and all the financial leverage required to fix this overnight.
Instead, Google treats the issue with a massive, corporate shrug. “Fixing” it is a conflict of interest: Every single time an intrusive, annoying ad successfully tricks a user into clicking it, money changes hands. And because Google is a dominant player in the mobile advertising space, a slice of that ad spend inevitably finds its way into Google’s pockets.
The proof is in the pudding, and the pudding tastes like malware.
To give credit where it’s due, Google claims it’s fighting the good fight. It publishes press releases highlighting how its AI defenses blocked millions of policy-violating apps or banned thousands of bad developer accounts. Google has also recently rolled out stricter developer verification programs. This is all well and good, and on the surface seems like a move in the right direction.
But anyone with an Android phone knows the truth: the proof is in the pudding, and the pudding tastes like malware. Google’s automated filters are clearly failing to catch the sheer volume of borderline-fraudulent advertising slipping through the cracks. It feels like Google only takes aggressive action when an ad is explicitly caught deploying literal spyware, while completely ignoring the ads that “merely” ruin the entire user experience. The financial gain from these spammy ads doesn’t give much incentive for change, either.
The road ahead
Android’s greatest strength has always been its openness and accessibility. It allowed anyone, anywhere, to pick up a budget device and have access to a world of free, innovative software.
But “free” shouldn’t mean “toxic.” By allowing the ad ecosystem to degenerate into its current state, Google is actively training users to distrust free apps entirely. If a platform becomes so frustrating to use that people are afraid to click on a standard utility app for fear of a full-screen, un-closable pop-up, that platform is fundamentally broken.
It’s time for the tech giant to stop sitting on its hands. Clean up the Play Store, ban predatory ad networks, and give us back the Android ecosystem we actually fell in love with.
articles.summary:
Google faces growing criticism for allowing predatory, intrusive advertising practices to dominate the Android Play Store, effectively degrading the user experience and stifling independent app development on the platform.

### Key Points
- Modern Android apps frequently utilize "dark patterns," including unclosable interstitial video ads, deceptive playable mini-games, and unauthorized notification spam.
- Aggressive ad SDKs from major brokers have become the primary revenue source for developers, as traditional banner ad payouts have significantly declined.
- Independent developers are increasingly leaving the ecosystem because they cannot compete with low-effort, ad-heavy "copycat" apps that prioritize data harvesting over utility.
- Critics argue that Google, as the owner of both the Android platform and the AdMob network, has a financial conflict of interest that discourages strict enforcement.
- While Google claims to use AI and developer verification to block policy-violating apps, users report that fraudulent and hostile advertising remains widespread.

### Why it Matters
The proliferation of toxic advertising is eroding user trust in the Android ecosystem, potentially rendering the platform's library of free utilities unusable. If Google fails to regulate these predatory practices, it risks permanently damaging the reputation of its mobile software marketplace and alienating its massive global user base.

articles.title: Files
articles.author: Paul Thurrott
articles.content:
File Explorer offers point-and-click access to the Windows 11 file system and deep integration with OneDrive, Microsoft’s cloud storage service.
{#fileexplorer}
When you buy a new PC or reset an existing PC, Windows 11 configures the file system like so:
You configure File Explorer in the app’s Folder Options dialog as described later in this chapter.
You configure OneDrive using the settings interface in the OneDrive app as described later in this chapter.
File Explorer is the Windows 11 file management app, and though it looks and works much like its predecessors in earlier Windows versions, it’s been modernized with a tabbed user interface, improved customization, and more.
There are shortcuts to File Explorer in the Pinned area in Start and on the Taskbar, and you can launch the app from those locations or using Windows Search.
Keyboard shortcut: You can also launch File Explorer by typing Windows key + E.
File Explorer displays a single tab open to the Home page by default. Key default features include:
The Windows 11 File Explorer displays its Home page by default. This page is somewhat unique in File Explorer in that it doesn’t correspond to a file system location but instead presents a simpler and potentially useful selection of frequently accessed folders and files.
The Home page displays the following sections by default:
File Explorer tabs work much like those in any web browser. Key features include:
Keyboard shortcut: You can also open a new tab by typing Ctrl + T.
Keyboard shortcut: You can also close a new tab by typing Ctrl + W.
Tip: If there is only a single tab displayed in File Explorer and you close that tab, the app exits.
Keyboard shortcut: You can also move from tab-to-tab by typing Ctrl + Tab. Or, to do so in reverse order, Ctrl + Shift + Tab.
Tip: Copying and moving between tabs follows the file copy/move rules you’re familiar with. If the two locations are in the same disk or partition, the default drag-and-drop operation is copy, but if they are on different disks or partitions, it’s move. If you wish to use the other, you can right-drag the items instead.
Keyboard shortcut: You can also use the Ctrl + C/Ctrl + X and Ctrl + V keyboard shortcuts to copy/cut the selected items in the source folder and then paste them in the destination folder, respectively.
File Explorer provides a modernized Address bar in Windows 11, which means that it’s been completely rewritten and offers a few subtle new features.
Among them are:
The File Explorer navigation pane provides three main sections from top-to-bottom:
While File Explorer works well for most users without modification, power users and others may wish to customize how it works.
By default, File Explorer opens to the Home page, which displays your Quick access folders, favorite files, recently accessed files, and more in different views. But you can customize the home page so that it opens to This PC or OneDrive if you prefer.
To do so, click “See more” (“…”) in the File Explorer command bar and then select “Options” to display the Folder Options window.
In the default General tab, select “Open File Explorer to:” to display the choices.
By default, Quick access displays shortcuts to your Desktop, Downloads, Documents, Pictures, Music, and Videos folders. But as you access other folders frequently, they will be added to Quick access automatically as well.
This behavior is also configured in Folder Options. You can find the relevant options in the Privacy section on the General tab, and they are all enabled by default.
They are:
File Explorer is configured for most mainstream users, meaning it hides some complexity by default. But more advanced users may wish to customize what File Explorer displays and how. There are two places in Windows 11 to do so.
The first, oddly, is in the Settings app: If you navigate to System > Advanced > File Explorer, you will find a page listing several of the most common File Explorer view options–Show file extensions, Show hidden and system files, and more–that advanced users often configure.
These and other File Explorer view options can also be configured in the File Explorer Folder Options dialog. Just navigate to View and take a look through the list of options in “Advanced settings.”
Tip: Unlike so many interfaces in Windows 11, this dialog includes a nice “Restore Defaults” button you can click if you made a view customization you regret and can’t figure out which option to revert.
You can also customize which items appear in the Navigation pane to some degree. To do so, open Folder Options, navigate to the View tab, and examine the options in “Advanced settings” under “Navigation pane.”
These include:
Tip: You can quickly access many of these toggles by right-clicking an empty area of the navigation bar.
When you connect a removable drive to your PC, one or more drive entries appear at the bottom of the File Explorer navigation pane, each with a corresponding drive letter. Here, you can see a single new entry, for “Media,” that was assigned to drive letter D.
You may find this useful, as this new entry gives you an obvious place to click if you’d like to see the contents of that drive. But you may instead find it superfluous and a waste of on-screen real estate. You can configure Windows to automatically open any newly attached removable drive in a File Explorer window. And you can always access the removable drive along with any other PC storage by navigating to the This PC view.
There’s no interface here or anywhere else in Windows 11 that lets you configure File Explorer to not display removable drives. But you can use the Registry to prevent removable drives from appearing in the File Explorer navigation pane.
To do so, open the Registry Editor with Windows Search and navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Desktop\NameSpace\DelegateFolders\{F5FB2C77-0E2F-4A16-A381-3E560C68BC83}. Right-click the “{F5FB2C77-0E2F-4A16-A381-3E560C68BC83}” key, choose “Rename,” and add just a single character to the beginning or end of that key and then tap Enter.
That’s it: The drive(s) will disappear from the navigation bar in File Explorer, but you’ll still be able to access them in the This PC view.
You can reverse the change by renaming that Registry key back to its original name.
Microsoft has added a new Details pane to File Explorer that displays a thumbnail or icon, plus meta-data for the currently selected file. The Details pane appears on the right side of the files area and is toggled on and off via a new button at the far right of the File Explorer command bar.
Keyboard shortcut: You can also type Alt + Shift + P to toggle the Details pane.
What you see in the Details pane will depend on the type of the selected file. There is a thumbnail preview for image files, for example, while many other file types will simply display an icon.
Tip: The Details pane works with individual files only. It can’t display information about two or more files.
Below that, you will find a “Share” button that duplicates the “Share” button in File Explorer’s command bar, some selection of meta-data about the file that also varies by type, and a “Properties” button that opens the selected file’s property sheet, a legacy interface for displaying file information.
File Explorer has long offered integrated support for the popular ZIP archive file format via a feature called compressed folders. This feature lets users compress (“zip”) one or more files into a ZIP archive and extract (“unzip”) files from a ZIP archive. But it also simplifies the use of ZIP archives by allowing them to work like normal folders: You can open a ZIP archive like a folder, browse its contents, copy or move content to or from the archive, and perform other basic file operations.
With Windows 11, Microsoft has extended compressed folders to support compressing and extracting other archive file formats. So you can now create, view, and extract TAR (.tar, .tar.gz, .tar.bz2, .tar.zst, .tar.xz, .tgz, .tbz2, .tzst, and .txz) and 7-ZIP (.7z) archive file formats–plus ZIP files, as before–without needing to install a third-party app.
To create a compressed archive file, select one or more files in File Explorer (or the Desktop), right-click, and then choose “Compress to…” and then the appropriate archive format.
When you make your choice, Windows will create the file in the current folder (or on the Desktop) and then prompt you to name it.
To open a compressed archive file, just double-click it to display its contents like a normal folder inside a File Explorer window.
To extract a compressed archive file, right-click it, select “Extract All” and then click “Extract” in the Extract Archive window that appears (after optionally changing the destination path).
You may sometimes want to format a fixed disk or removable drive, a process that deletes any data on the device and prepares it for use with a clean, empty file system. There are various ways to do this, but the simplest by far is to use File Explorer.
Tip: You can’t format the system disk where Windows resides, typically the C: drive. You also can’t use File Explorer to format hidden partitions on any disk; for that, you must use Disk Management.
To format a disk, open File Explorer and navigate to This PC. Then, right-click the disk and choose “Format…” from the context menu that appears to display the “Format” dialog.
The following options are relevant here:
When you’re ready, click “Start” to format the disk.
OneDrive is Microsoft’s cloud storage service, and it is deeply integrated with Windows 11, allowing users to automatically sync content between the cloud and their PCs through File Explorer.
Tip: There are also OneDrive apps on the web, Android devices, iPhones and iPad, and Macs.
OneDrive runs when Windows 11 starts up so that you can seamlessly access the OneDrive app and the file system integration in File Explorer. The default configuration can vary based on choices you make during the Windows Setup Out-of-Box Experience (OOBE) and whether you sign in to Windows 11 using a Microsoft account (MSA) (or a Microsoft Work or School account, or MWSA).
If you sign in to Windows 11 with one of those online accounts, you may be prompted to “back up your files to OneDrive” during the OOBE, a reference to the OneDrive Folder backup feature. If you agreed to this offer, then Windows 11 enabled Folder backup for your Desktop, Documents, and Pictures folders.
If you did not agree to that offer or were not prompted with it, OneDrive may still attempt to automatically enable Folder backup during the first several minutes after you finish the OOBE in which it is indexing your cloud storage contents to make it fully visible in File Explorer. As discussed in Initial Configuration and Personalization, it’s possible to short circuit that if you know how and move quickly enough. But if you’re reading this now, it’s too late, and you have to look at OneDrive settings to see how things are configured and change certain settings if desired.
Given all that:
OneDrive customization occurs in two places: OneDrive app settings and File Explorer.
To get started, find and click the OneDrive system tray icon to display the OneDrive app. Then, click “Help & settings” (gear) and then “Settings” to display OneDrive settings.
There’s a lot here. But key settings across each major page of this settings interface are as follows:
OneDrive is deeply integrated with File Explorer in ways both obvious and not. Some key configurations here include:
Tip: If OneDrive Folder backup is enabled, then anything stored in your Desktop, Documents, and Pictures folders is likewise available when the PC is offline.
Windows 11 places your OneDrive folder inside your user account folder structure (C:\Users\username\OneDrive by default). This isn’t something you need to think about all that often, since File Explorer provides a direct link to OneDrive in its navigation bar and simplifies the file path display in its Address bar.
But you can move OneDrive to a new location if you’d like. For example, those with desktop PCs sometimes have two drives, with a solid-state drive as the system disk (C:) and a large, traditional hard drive (D:) that’s used for data. You may wish to move OneDrive from the system drive (C:) to that larger (D:) drive.
To do so, open OneDrive settings and navigate to the Account tab. Then, click the “Unlink this PC” link. Windows will prompt you to explain what will happen if you proceed.
Click “Unlink account.” A “Set up OneDrive” window will appear.
Enter the email address for your Microsoft account as prompted and handle any two-step authentication requirements as prompted. Then, step through a series of mostly promotional windows until you arrive at one titled “Your OneDrive folder.”
Here, you will find a link, “Change location,” that lets you choose a new location for the OneDrive folder. Simply use the “Choose your OneDrive location” window–a renamed Open file dialog–to choose a new location.
Tip: A OneDrive folder will be created for you in whatever location you choose. That is, you don’t need to create a OneDrive folder manually.
After this, you will need to step through several other windows in which you can configure OneDrive Folder backup and then learn more about OneDrive. When you’re done, File Explorer will open to the local version of your OneDrive, which is now in its new location in your PC’s file system.
Tip: The original OneDrive folder will remain on disk on your PC after this change, and it will contain any files you downloaded or synced to this PC. You can leave it there, of course, but if you delete it, it will free up whatever storage space it is taking up.
When enabled, OneDrive Folder backup is configured to back up three key user folders–Desktop, Documents, and Pictures–to OneDrive. It does this by redirecting these local file system locations to similar locations inside the OneDrive folder structure. That is, instead of using C:\Users\user-name\Desktop as the location for your Desktop folder, it will use C:\Users\user-name\OneDrive\Desktop.
Tip: You can also optionally back up (sync) the Music and Videos user folders to OneDrive as well.
OneDrive Folder backup is incredibly useful if you store any documents or other files in any or all of those folders because they will be synced to OneDrive in the cloud and then made available to you on any PC, Mac, phone, or other device with access to the web or a OneDrive app. But this takes up storage as well, and if you’re not paying for additional OneDrive storage, typically through a consumer Microsoft 365 subscription, you can quickly fill up the free 5 GB that Microsoft provides to everyone.
The issues with OneDrive Folder backup don’t end there, however. Some may not want to back up these folders to OneDrive because they don’t want to use up too much of their OneDrive storage allotment, don’t use those folders anyway, or use other cloud storage services. And Windows 11 has a nasty habit of auto-enabling this feature in some circumstances, even after you’ve declined an offer to do so.
However your feelings about OneDrive Folder backup, you should at least examine how this feature is configured. And, if desired, enable or disable the automatic backup (sync) of one or more of those folders.
To do so, open OneDrive settings . In the Sync and backup page, click the “Manage backup” button to display the “Back up folders on this PC” window.
Here, you will see On/Off toggle controls for the three folder locations that OneDrive automatically backs up–Documents, Pictures, and Desktop–plus two other important folders in your user account, Music and Videos. You can toggle each folder as you wish and then click “Save changes” or “Close” as needed.
Note, however, that making changes here will trigger certain behaviors and some are new to 2026.
If you enable Folder backup on one or more of these folders, OneDrive will display a window called “OneDrive is backing up your files.” You can just close this window and get back to work, or you can click “View progress” to watch the files sync in the main OneDrive window.
In each case, Windows 11 will do the following:
Tip: That last point is important. You will likely have content in the OneDrive version(s) of the folder(s) already, and when you or the system enables OneDrive Folder backup, that content will now include whatever was in the local version(s) of the folder(s) too.
Disabling OneDrive Folder backup is more complicated. The issue is that you used to have two different folders–say, the local Documents folder associated with your sign-in account and the Documents folder in OneDrive–but you now have a single folder–the Documents folder in OneDrive, in this example–that’s storing the combined contents of what was previously in both folders. So when you disable OneDrive Folder backup, that content–which, again, is the combined contents from two locations–has to go somewhere.
Previously, when you disabled OneDrive Folder backup on a folder, its contents would remain in OneDrive. That is, if you were using OneDrive Folder backup with the Documents folder but then disabled it, the following would happen:
New to 2026, this behavior has changed. Now, when you disable OneDrive Folder backup on a folder, you’re given two choices.
Easy, right?
Wrong. Unless you’ve only used a single computer over the past several years, it’s possible–in fact, it’s highly likely–that the OneDrive version of whatever folder had content in it from those other PCs, all commingled together. And it could be a lot of files that take up a lot of disk space, so choose carefully: The interface you’re looking at explains how many files will transfer and how much space they require locally.
Tip: If you’re not sure what to do, choose “Only in OneDrive” here and then use File Explorer–perhaps two File Explorer windows side-by-side to determine which if any of the files in the OneDrive version of the folder you wish to copy or move back to the local file system version of the folder.
Before cloud computing, we saved documents and other files in the file systems of our PCs and hopefully backed them up from time to time using a removable drive or network-attached storage device. But with OneDrive and other cloud-based storage services, a more reliable and secure strategy emerged. Now, we can automatically sync the documents and other files we work with to the cloud, ensuring that they are, in effect, backed up to a different geographical location and, as good, be easily accessible on our other PCs and compatible devices.
Looking at OneDrive specifically, Microsoft’s cloud storage service deeply integrates with Windows 11 using a feature called Files on Demand, as noted above. But you aren’t limited to syncing your Desktop, Documents, and Pictures folders–and, optionally, your Music and Videos folders–with OneDrive. In Windows 11, your entire OneDrive is accessible in the file system and thus from File Explorer and any other app that deals with files. And you can arbitrarily configure individual files or folders full of files to sync between OneDrive and your PC using another useful OneDrive feature called Files on Demand.
To understand this better, open File Explorer and click the OneDrive (“username – Personal”) item in the top section of the Navigation bar on the left. When you do, the contents of your OneDrive storage appear in the app.
By default–and except for the content in folders impacted by OneDrive Folder backup if that feature is enabled–any OneDrive-based files that you view using File Explorer are not actually on your PC. Instead, they are in the cloud and Windows displays lightweight placeholders of those files, or stubs, which include thumbnails in many cases, and even detailed file information.
In the language of Files On-Demand, these files are said to be online-only files. They are visible in your PC’s file system. But they are not stored in their original form in the file system. This means they are not available when your PC is offline.
Consider the Properties dialog for an online-only file. As you can see, its size is 2.9 MB; this represents how much storage the file actually takes up. But its size on disk is (roughly) 0 bytes. It takes up almost no space at all. That’s the magic of Files On-Demand: You can see the OneDrive-based file in File Explorer, but it’s taking up almost no space unless you need to open it.
The downside to online-only files is that they are only viewable and accessible in the file system when the PC is online. But you can make them accessible in the file system even when the PC is offline if desired. Even better, this system keeps the files synced with OneDrive in the cloud. In the language of Files On-Demand, such files are said to be always available files.
There are two ways to do this:
You can also reverse the changes described above. To remove the local version of an always available file and free up some local storage space, right-click it and choose “Free up space” from the context menu that appears.
Tip: This also works with multiple selected files at once and with entire folders.
As you work with OneDrive files in File Explorer, you will notice that all OneDrive folder views display a Status indicator icon for each file in that folder. There are three possible icon overlays, and each visually helps you quickly determine their offline availability.
They are:
OneDrive lets you securely store your most important or sensitive documents, photos, and other files in a specially protected area called Personal Vault. Personal Vault is a top-level folder location in OneDrive, and it is accessible via the web, the OneDrive app on mobile, the Finder on the Mac, and File Explorer in Windows 11. But it differs from other OneDrive folders in that it requires you to verify your identity when you access it.
Tip: That verification will be familiar to you if you use your Microsoft account’s two-step verification protections, as you should.
Tip: All Microsoft account holders can store 3 files in Personal Vault, but if you have a Microsoft 365 account, there is no file limit.
Before you can use the OneDrive Personal Vault in Windows 11, you have to enable it. To do so, open File Explorer, navigate to OneDrive, and double-click the Personal Vault item as you would any other folder. Windows 11 will prompt you about this feature, explaining how it works.
Click “Next,” then click “Allow” when prompted. Windows will display a User Account Control (UAC) permissions dialog. Approve that request.
After a bit of work, another prompt will appear asking you to sign in to your Microsoft account using a two-step verification request that will be delivered to your phone. Complete that process, and the Personal Vault folder will open in File Explorer. It will be empty if you’ve never used it before
Once Personal Vault is enabled on your PC, you can use it normally and much like any other folder. That is, you can copy or move files or folders into or out of Personal Vault, delete files or folders in Personal Vault, or perform any other standard file operations.
There is one security-related wrinkle: You will be prompted to verify your identity from time to time. This can happen if you’ve not used Personal Vault in a while. But it can also happen when you have been using Personal Vault but 20 minutes have elapsed since the last access.
Files copied or moved into Personal Vault can of course be viewed, edited, and deleted from the web or the OneDrive mobile app as well. In both cases, you will be prompted to verify your identity each time.
File Explorer lets you share files with others using email, Nearby share, compatible apps, and other means. But when the files you wish to share are stored in OneDrive, the experience changes. That’s because OneDrive doesn’t need to use your PC as an intermediary: Instead of manually sending these files to others, you can create a link for them to use so they can access the files directly from OneDrive. This link will let the other person view the file(s) in OneDrive in the cloud (in a web browser), download them, and, if allowed, even edit them.
To see this, select one or more OneDrive-based files and/or folders with File Explorer and then click the “Share” icon in the app’s command bar.
Tip: You can also right-click the selected files/folders and click the “Share” icon in the pop-up menu that appears.
The “Share link” window appears.
Here, you can choose how to send the link and configure permissions for the shared file(s). Available options include:
Tip: Allowing a recipient to edit a shared file isn’t as dangerous as it sounds: The recipients can edit the file(s) you shared in OneDrive, but those changes will be saved to a copy in their OneDrive and won’t impact the original.
The people who receive the link can access the files using their web browser (after entering the correct password, if you specify that).
Everyone with a Microsoft account gets 5 GB of free OneDrive storage space. But you can pay more to get more storage, starting with a Microsoft 365 subscription: Those with a Microsoft 365 Basic ($1.99 per month or $19.99 per year) subscription get an additional 100 GB of storage, while those with Microsoft 365 Personal ($6.99 per month or $69.99 per year), Family ($9.99 per month or $99.99 per year), or Premium ($19.99 per month or $199 per year) subscriptions get an additional 1 TB of OneDrive storage per user.
Once you have a Microsoft 365 subscription, you can add additional storage in 200 GB ($1.99 per month), 1 TB ($9.99 per month), 5 TB ($49.99 per month), and 10 TB ($99.99 per month) allotments if 1 TB isn’t enough.
If you don’t use OneDrive, you can disable or even uninstall it. Either option will prevent the app from auto-starting when you sign in to Windows, and you won’t see OneDrive entries in File Explorer or a OneDrive icon in the system tray in the Taskbar.
To disable OneDrive, open OneDrive settings, navigate to the Account page, and click the “Unlink this PC” link next to your profile picture. Then, in the “Unlink account on this PC?” window that appears, click “Unlink account.” The window will close, you will be signed out of OneDrive.
Now, click the OneDrive icon in the system tray. In the OneDrive window that appears–it’s now titled “OneDrive isn’t connected”– click the Help & Settings (gear) icon and then select “Quit OneDrive” from the menu that appears. You will be prompted one last time to make sure you’re serious.
When you click “Quit OneDrive,” OneDrive will finally close.
But you’re not done yet: You have a few more tasks to complete.
Tip: Remember, the files it contains are all synced to the cloud. So you aren’t deleting the originals.
If disabling OneDrive isn’t drastic enough, you can uninstall it instead. All you have to do is open the Settings app, navigate to Apps > Installed apps, locate “Microsoft OneDrive” in the alphabetical list of installed apps, click its “More options” (“…”) link and then select “Uninstall” from the menu that appears.
You’ll be asked if you’re sure, and then you’ll need to approve a User Account Control (UAC) prompt. When you do, OneDrive is uninstalled.
As with the disable method described above, you should delete the OneDrive folder from your user profile–it’s in C:\Users\user-name–and then reboot the PC.
Tip: If you change your mind later and wish to continue using OneDrive, you can download it from the Microsoft website and then reinstall it.
With technology shaping our everyday lives, how could we not dig deeper?
Thurrott Premium delivers an honest and thorough perspective about the technologies we use and rely on everyday. Discover deeper content as a Premium member.
articles.summary:
Windows 11 File Explorer provides a modernized, tabbed interface for managing local files and deep cloud integration with Microsoft OneDrive to streamline storage, synchronization, and secure data sharing.

### Key Points
- File Explorer features a tabbed interface, a customizable Home page, and a new Details pane for viewing file metadata.
- Native support now includes creating and extracting ZIP, TAR, and 7-ZIP archive formats without third-party software.
- OneDrive integration enables "Files On-Demand," allowing users to access cloud files without occupying local disk space.
- Users can manage folder backups for Desktop, Documents, and Pictures, or secure sensitive data using the Personal Vault.
- Advanced customization options are available via the Folder Options dialog or the Windows Settings app for power users.
- OneDrive can be unlinked or uninstalled through system settings if users prefer to manage files exclusively on local storage.

### Why it Matters
Understanding File Explorer’s integration with OneDrive is essential for managing storage efficiency and ensuring data remains accessible across multiple devices. Mastering these tools allows users to optimize their workflow, secure sensitive information, and maintain control over how their files are synced and stored.

articles.title: 'It almost defied belief': here are the 5 best OnePlus phones ever, as the 'flagship killer' leaves the US and Europe
articles.author: David Nield
articles.content:
After months of speculation, it's official: parent company Oppo is pulling OnePlus devices out of US and European markets. While existing customers are still going to get after-sales support and software updates from OnePlus, we're not going to see any more new phones, tablets, or smartwatches in these regions in the future.
Oppo is branding this as a "strategic" move, but the decision is based on simple economics. OnePlus has always struggled to maintain the all-important carrier relationships in the US, and is being squeezed by Samsung, Apple, Google, and Chinese manufacturers in terms of sales.
We're not here to dissect what went wrong in this particular article though — we're here to celebrate some of the fantastic smartphones that OnePlus has made down the years, since the company was founded back in 2013. There have been dozens of OnePlus handsets launched since then, but these are our five favorites (in order of release).
1. OnePlus One (2014)
We had to start with the OnePlus One really, as it set the tone for OnePlus, and epitomized everything the fledgling company was about. Boldly marketed as a "flagship killer" and with the tagline "never settle" — as in, never settle for a poor compromise between price and performance — it really did shake up the market in a significant way.
Our review praises the value-for-money proposition — it was less than half the price of the flagship Samsung Galaxy, at the time — as well as the top-notch specs (a Snapdragon 801 and 3GB of RAM) and the customization features available in CyanogenMod, which is the operating system that the first OnePlus phone came running.
It almost defied belief that a phone this well made, with such good internal specs, could cost this little — and from nowhere, OnePlus was suddenly on the scene in a big way. As a historical note, it's also funny looking back to see the 5.5-inch screen described as being relatively big, in 2014 terms.
2. OnePlus 7 Pro (2019)
There were some excellent handsets between the debut phone from OnePlus and the OnePlus 7 Pro in 2019 — it was hardly a fallow time for the company — but this was the first handset to really wow us since the OnePlus One. That was partly down to its great party trick: a pop-up selfie camera.
Sign up for breaking news, reviews, opinion, top tech deals, and more.
Thanks to that pop-up camera, there was no need for a notch or cut-out on the front screen, and it looked gorgeous from the front (and the back, as it happens). It was a phone that genuinely turned heads when you took it out of your pocket, and our review waxed lyrical about the performance levels and charging speeds, too.
By this stage, OnePlus wasn't quite giving us the same jaw-dropping value for money with its flagship phones as it did with the OnePlus One, but this phone still held its own in terms of pricing with the best flagships on the market in 2019. The screen had been upped to 6.67 inches in size, and — notably — boasted a 90 Hz refresh rate too.
3. OnePlus Open (2023)
You could make a strong argument that despite the best efforts of Samsung and Google, OnePlus was the first phone maker to get a foldable phone right in every department: the OnePlus Open was awarded 4.5 stars out of 5 in its TechRadar review, with the biggest pros listed as the displays, the cameras, and the thin and light build.
As we put it at the time: "The OnePlus Open is the only big foldable phone that doesn’t feel like a compromise. It looks and feels like a normal phone, and the camera is the best you'll find on any foldable." Of course, it was still expensive (what book-style foldable isn't), but this was finally a folding phone that could justify the cost.
OnePlus was able to improve on what had gone before in this form factor in multiple areas, including the foldable's hinge and crease, and it still managed to score highly in all the categories that matter on any type of smartphone. Meanwhile, the OxygenOS software responded nimbly and seamlessly to the folding design too.
4. OnePlus 13 (2024)
Admittedly our list is a little biased towards later models: that's no criticism of the many fantastic earlier handsets, but OnePlus really has been hitting it out of the park in the last couple of years. As our reviewer for the OnePlus 13 put it: "I'm dumbfounded, I can't find anything wrong with this phone."
It had a huge 6,000 mAh battery, it ran super-smooth thanks to its Snapdragon 8 Elite processor, and it had an IP69 rating — a first for a OnePlus phone. The handset also boasted a gorgeous 6.82-inch display, and powerful triple-lens rear cameras. This handset was a real return to top-tier OnePlus.
You can probably guess what the main downside was for customers in the US — it wasn't possible to buy the OnePlus 13 in local carrier stores, which didn't help with brand awareness and recognition. Looking back, this may be one of the phones that made up Oppo's mind about exiting international markets: it was a device that deserved to sell in much higher numbers than it actually did.
5. OnePlus 15 (2025)
We'll close out this list with last year's OnePlus 15, which earned a rare 5-stars-out-of-5 ranking in its TechRadar review. There was so much to love about this phone: the battery life, the performance, the design, the software, the durability... it basically checked all of the boxes you would want, and there was really nothing bad to say about it.
The phone also shows that OnePlus was making great handsets right up until the end (in the US and Europe). It was other issues that were the problem — again, the OnePlus 15 couldn't be found inside carrier stores in the United States, which meant it wasn't as visible to consumers, and you had to pay more up front to get it.
While this wasn't quite the last OnePlus phone to launch internationally, it still stands as a testament to the quality of the handsets the company has put out down the years. It more than holds its own against anything Apple, Google, and Samsung have ever made, and it's a shame that most of the world won't see these handsets any more.
Follow TechRadar on Google News and add us as a preferred source to get our expert news, reviews, and opinion in your feeds.
Dave is a freelance tech journalist who has been writing about gadgets, apps and the web for more than two decades. Based out of Stockport, England, on TechRadar you'll find him covering news, features and reviews, particularly for phones, tablets and wearables. Working to ensure our breaking news coverage is the best in the business over weekends, David also has bylines at Gizmodo, T3, PopSci and a few other places besides, as well as being many years editing the likes of PC Explorer and The Hardware Handbook.
